event: Early modern humans inhabit what is now Laos, with skeletal remains found at Tam Pa Ling cave among the oldest in mainland Southeast Asia.
Bronze Age communities develop in the region, part of broader prehistoric metallurgy traditions in mainland Southeast Asia.
Iron Age cultures emerge, and the megalithic stone vessels of the Plain of Jars begin to be created on the Xiangkhoang Plateau.
Mon Dvaravati Buddhist culture spreads influence into parts of the middle Mekong region.
Muang Sua (later Luang Prabang) and other early Tai-Lao principalities form along the Mekong as Khmer influence extends northward.
Tai peoples expand across the region as the Sukhothai kingdom rises, accelerating Tai migration and statehood in present-day Laos.
Fa Ngum founds the unified kingdom of Lan Xang ('Land of a Million Elephants') with its capital at Muang Sua, establishing the first great Lao state.
Theravada Buddhism is established as the state religion of Lan Xang, and the sacred Phra Bang Buddha image is brought from the Khmer court.
King Samsenthai succeeds Fa Ngum and consolidates Lan Xang's administration, conducting a census that gives him his name ('300,000 Tai').
Lan Xang enters a period of dynastic instability and succession disputes following the death of King Samsenthai.
Vietnamese forces under Le Thanh Tong invade and sack the Lan Xang capital before being driven out, marking a major external threat.
King Photisarath ascends the throne and strengthens Buddhism while expanding Lan Xang's regional influence.
King Setthathirath ascends, briefly uniting Lan Xang with Lan Na (Chiang Mai) through dynastic ties.
Setthathirath moves the capital from Luang Prabang to Vientiane to better defend against Burmese expansion.
King Setthathirath builds the That Luang stupa in Vientiane, which becomes the most sacred national monument of Laos.
King Sourigna Vongsa begins a long reign regarded as the golden age of Lan Xang, marked by peace, prosperity, and cultural flourishing.
Dutch merchant Gerrit van Wuysthoff of the East India Company visits Vientiane, leaving the first European account of Lan Xang.
Lan Xang fractures following Sourigna Vongsa's death, splitting into the rival kingdoms of Luang Prabang and Vientiane.
The southern Kingdom of Champasak breaks away, dividing the Lao lands into three competing kingdoms.
Siamese forces conquer Vientiane and Champasak, reducing the Lao kingdoms to Siamese vassal states.
King Anouvong of Vientiane leads a rebellion against Siamese domination, which ultimately fails.
Siam razes Vientiane and deports much of its population, ending the Kingdom of Vientiane as an independent entity.
France establishes a vice-consulate in Luang Prabang, beginning direct French involvement in Lao affairs.
After the Franco-Siamese War, Siam cedes Lao territory east of the Mekong to France, creating a French protectorate over Laos.
France consolidates the Lao territories into a single administrative unit within French Indochina.
A Franco-Siamese treaty fixes the borders of French Laos, defining much of the modern territory of the country.
Japanese forces occupy Laos during World War II, and Prince Phetsarath proclaims the Lao Issara independence movement.
France reasserts control and reunifies Laos under the king of Luang Prabang as a constitutional monarchy within the French Union.
Laos is granted limited self-government as an associated state within the French Union.
Laos gains full independence from France under the Kingdom of Laos with King Sisavang Vong as monarch.
The Geneva Accords confirm Lao independence after the First Indochina War, but leave the communist Pathet Lao controlling two northern provinces.
Laos is admitted as a member state of the United Nations.
A first coalition government unites royalists and the Pathet Lao under Prince Souvanna Phouma in an attempt at national reconciliation.
Captain Kong Le stages a coup seeking neutrality, deepening the civil war among rightist, neutralist, and communist factions.
A second Geneva agreement declares Lao neutrality, but it quickly breaks down as fighting resumes.
The United States begins a massive secret bombing campaign over Laos targeting the Ho Chi Minh Trail during the Vietnam War.
A ceasefire agreement ends overt fighting, and a third coalition government is formed including the Pathet Lao.
The Pathet Lao seize power, abolish the monarchy, and proclaim the Lao People's Democratic Republic under communist rule.
Laos signs a 25-year Treaty of Friendship with Vietnam, cementing close political and military ties.
The government launches the 'New Economic Mechanism,' beginning market-oriented reforms and opening to foreign investment.
Laos adopts its first constitution as a one-party socialist republic led by the Lao People's Revolutionary Party.
The town of Luang Prabang is designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its preserved royal and Buddhist architecture.
Laos becomes a full member of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN).
The Vat Phou ancient Khmer temple complex in southern Laos is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Laos joins the World Trade Organization, integrating further into the global economy.
The Plain of Jars megalithic landscape is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
The China-Laos Railway opens, linking Vientiane to Kunming and marking a major Belt and Road infrastructure milestone.
Laos assumes the rotating chairmanship of ASEAN amid efforts to manage mounting public debt and regional diplomacy.
